# WASDE Countdown: Futures Selloff, Dollar Surge, and a Forecasting Reckoning

*With corn futures down 12% from their May peak and a surging dollar squeezing export competitiveness, Tuesday's WASDE will either validate or gut the most bullish U.S. corn export projection in five years.*

## Lead: The June 9 WASDE Arrives Into a Market Already in Retreat

Corn July futures settled at 417.5¢/bu on June 5, down 57.75¢ from their May 19 peak of 475.25¢/bu, a 12.1% decline in under three weeks. Soybean July futures fell 88.0¢ over the same span to 1,121.5¢/bu, a 7.3% drop. The selloff did not happen in a vacuum. The U.S. Dollar Index closed at 120.08 on June 5, its highest reading in the trailing 30-day window and up from 118.73 on April 24, a gain of roughly 1.35 points that directly pressures dollar-denominated export competitiveness. Into this environment, USDA drops its June WASDE on Tuesday, June 9.

The single number that matters most is the 2025-26 corn export line. The April 18 WASDE pegged it at 83,824,000 MT, a 15.5% jump above the 2024-25 final of 72,603,000 MT (USDA WASDE April release). That projection would be the largest U.S. corn export figure in at least the recent marketing years tracked by USDA, surpassing the 2024-25 final and well above the 2020-21 marketing year total of 69,775,000 MT. The bar USDA set for itself in April is historically high, and the market has been pricing in the risk that it cannot be maintained.

{{chart_1}}

The pace data offer a mixed read. As of the April 2 export-inspections week, corn's combined accumulated exports plus outstanding sales totaled approximately 71.4 million MT, roughly 85% of the full-year WASDE target. On the surface, that is on track. But the weekly booking pace has deteriorated sharply. Corn weekly net sales peaked at 4,010,632 MT in the week ending January 15, then decelerated to 1,361,349 MT by April 2, a 66% drop in weekly booking pace that coincides precisely with the DXY's climb from approximately 118 to above 120. A stronger dollar raises the cost of U.S. grain for foreign buyers, and the deceleration in new sales is consistent with that mechanism playing out in real time.

The stakes of any downward revision extend beyond the export line itself. The 2025-26 corn stocks-to-use ratio already stands at 0.6445 in the last WASDE, up from the tight 0.5427 of 2024-25 (USDA WASDE April release). Any trim to the export projection pushes ending stocks further into comfortable territory and adds bearish weight to a futures market already under pressure. The bull case for corn this marketing year has rested almost entirely on the export thesis. If USDA blinks on that number Tuesday, the structural support beneath current prices weakens considerably.

Soybeans carry their own vulnerability. Outstanding soybean sales had already shrunk from 12,360,464 MT in late January to just 7,389,139 MT by April 2, a 40% drawdown in the forward book. That leaves soybeans more exposed than corn to a demand-shock revision, with less of a cushion in the pipeline to absorb cancellations.

The June WASDE also introduces the first full new-crop supply estimates of the season, meaning Tuesday's report is not just a demand-side update. It is the opening statement on 2026-27 balance sheets, arriving at a moment when crop emergence conditions remain uncertain and the dollar is working against every export assumption USDA made in April.

Watch whether USDA revises the 2025-26 corn export line above or below 83,824,000 MT on June 9, and track the DXY simultaneously: a dollar holding above 120 paired with even a modest export cut would likely accelerate the futures selloff and confirm the bearish narrative heading into pollination season.

## Briefs

### North Carolina Corn: When a 300% Model Miss Is a Regional Pattern, Not an Outlier

In 2024, a yield forecasting model projected 126.0 bu/ac for FIPS 37063 in North Carolina. The county harvested 30.8 bu/ac, a 309% overestimation error and a 95.2 bu/ac gap. That miss is not idiosyncratic. Across 37 distinct North Carolina counties, the model posted errors exceeding 100% on 2024 corn, with an average overestimate of 170.7% and actual yields ranging from only 26.2 to 85.6 bu/ac. The worst single-county error belongs to neighboring FIPS 37101, where the model projected 139.6 bu/ac against an actual of 32.0 bu/ac, a 336% overshoot. For context, the model's average absolute error across 768 non-NC counties was just 20.2%, making the North Carolina cluster's misses roughly 15 times worse than the national norm. The county's own prior year (122.9 bu/ac in 2023 for FIPS 37063) gave the model no reason to expect collapse, but a 74.9% year-over-year decline arrived anyway. Watch whether 2025 model vintages for these same 37 NC counties have been recalibrated downward from their 2024 baselines before pollination forecasts solidify.

### Oklahoma Soybeans: The Overestimation Problem Crosses State Lines and Crops

Garfield County, Oklahoma (FIPS 40047) harvested 8.7 bu/ac of soybeans in 2024. The model's final-vintage p50 was 28.0 bu/ac, a 221.6% overestimation, meaning the model called more than three times what the field delivered. Critically, this was not a late-season surprise: across all 2024 forecast vintages logged for FIPS 40047, the model's p50 ranged from 28.0 to 30.7 bu/ac while the actual never moved. The 8.7 bu/ac result is the lowest on record for the county in available history, down from 13.1 bu/ac in 2023, 12.5 bu/ac in 2022, and less than one-third of the 28.2 bu/ac recorded in 2020. The county's 4-year average (2020-2024) sits at 15.6 bu/ac, and the 2024 result is 44% below even that depressed recent mean. The 2025 model for FIPS 40047 already carries a p50 of 27.5 bu/ac (p10: 22.5, p90: 35.6), essentially identical to the 2024 pre-season call that missed by 221%. Across all of 2024, 74 county-crop pairs across 70 counties and 2 crops posted errors exceeding 100%, confirming this failure mode spans geographies and commodities. Watch whether updated June model vintages for FIPS 40047 show any downward recalibration as emergence-season stress data begins flowing in.
